content:
# Genesis Chapter 3 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 2v–3r | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_003_page_001.jpg, GEN_003_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX, Folios 0006–0007 | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** Now the serpent was the most cunning of all the wild animals the LORD God had made. It said to the woman: "Did God really say you must not eat from any tree in the garden?"

**2** The woman said to the serpent, "We may eat from the trees of the garden —

**3** but from the tree in the middle of the garden, God said, 'You must not eat from it, and you must not touch it, or you will die.'"

**4** "You will not die," the serpent said to the woman.

**5** "God knows that the day you eat from it, your eyes will be opened, and you will be like God — knowing good and evil."

**6** The woman saw that the tree was good for food, that it was a delight to the eyes, and that it was desirable for gaining wisdom. She took some of its fruit and ate. She also gave some to her husband, who was with her, and he ate.

**7** Then the eyes of both of them were opened, and they realized they were naked. They sewed fig leaves together and made coverings for themselves.

**8** When the cool of the day came, they heard the sound of the LORD God walking about in the garden. The man and his wife hid themselves from the LORD God among the trees of the garden.

**9** The LORD God called to the man: "Where are you?"

**10** He said, "I heard your sound in the garden, and I was afraid because I was naked — so I hid."

**11** God said, "Who told you that you were naked? Have you eaten from the tree I commanded you not to eat from?"

**12** The man said, "The woman you gave to be with me — she gave me from the tree, and I ate."

**13** Then the LORD God said to the woman, "What is this you have done?" The woman said, "The serpent deceived me, and I ate."

**14** So the LORD God said to the serpent:

> "Because you have done this,
> cursed are you above all livestock
> and above all wild animals.
> On your belly you will crawl,
> and dust you will eat
> all the days of your life.

**15** I will put hostility between you and the woman,
> and between your offspring and hers.
> He will crush your head,
> and you will strike his heel."

**16** To the woman he said:

> "I will make your labor in childbirth severe —
> in pain you will bring forth children.
> Your desire will be for your husband,
> and he will rule over you."

**17** To the man he said:

> "Because you listened to your wife
> and ate from the tree about which I commanded you,
> 'You must not eat from it' —
> cursed is the ground because of you.
> Through hard toil you will eat from it
> all the days of your life.

**18** It will sprout thorns and thistles for you,
> and you will eat the plants of the field.

**19** By the sweat of your brow
> you will eat your bread,
> until you return to the ground —
> for from it you were taken.
> For you are dust,
> and to dust you will return."

**20** The man named his wife Eve, because she was the mother of all the living.

**21** The LORD God made garments of skin for the man and his wife, and he clothed them.

**22** Then the LORD God said, "Look — the human has become like one of us, knowing good and evil. And now, what if he reaches out his hand and also takes from the tree of life and eats, and lives forever?"

**23** So the LORD God sent him out from the garden of Eden, to work the ground from which he had been taken.

**24** He drove the man out, and stationed the cherubim to the east of the garden of Eden, along with the flaming, turning sword, to guard the way to the tree of life.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### On the dramatic register of this chapter

Genesis 3 is the pivot on which the entire biblical narrative turns. Everything before it was preparation; everything after it is consequence. The Hebrew text is taut, economical, and devastating — it moves with the precision of a blade. My goal throughout has been to match that pace: no padding, no softening, no homiletic editorializing. The drama must be allowed to do its own work. Where I made choices that go beyond the surface, I explain them below.

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 2v–3r, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Three-column format with full Tiberian Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. Additional reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Folios 0006–0007, Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana; Dead Sea Scrolls, 4QGen-b.*

---

### Verse 1 — "the most cunning" for עָרוּם (arum)

The Hebrew עָרוּם (arum) means shrewd, clever, crafty. It is the word used positively of the wise person in Proverbs. The serpent is not simply wicked — it is the cleverest thing in the garden. The word's positive register in Proverbs makes the serpent's presence here more unsettling, not less: intelligence itself has turned against the humans. "Cunning" carries the necessary edge of that intelligence turned manipulative. "Crafty" and "shrewd" are both defensible; I prefer "cunning" because it carries the combination of intelligence and predatory intent that the narrative requires.

The same Hebrew consonants (ayin-resh-vav-mem) that spell עָרוּם (arum, cunning) also spell עֲרוּמִּים (arummim, naked) — the word used at the end of chapter 2 ("they were both naked"). The Hebrew reader catches this immediately: the man and woman are naked (arummim) and the serpent is cunning (arum). The chapter begins on a word that is at once an echo and an irony. My translation cannot reproduce this, but I note it here because it is the text's first signal that something is about to go badly wrong.

The LXX Vaticanus reads **φρονιμώτατος** — "most prudent/sensible" — which preserves the positive-register force of arum. The serpent is not described as evil. It is described as the wisest creature in the garden. The horror is in the misuse.

---

### Verse 1 — "Did God really say...?" — the serpent's opening move

The serpent does not begin with a lie. It begins with a question that subtly misrepresents what God said. God said "any tree of the garden" in the negative: "you may eat from any tree" with one exception. The serpent inverts this: "Did God say you must not eat from *any* tree?" This is a deliberate distortion — converting a single specific prohibition into a seemingly total one — designed to provoke the woman into defending God and in the process clarifying and elaborating on the prohibition. The serpent is not interested in the answer. It is interested in what the woman will reveal in giving one.

I render the serpent's question with the opening "Did God *really* say" to capture the slight incredulity, the raised eyebrow. The Hebrew אַף כִּי (af ki) is a particle combination that introduces the serpent's words with an edge of surprise or irony — not simply "Did God say?" but something closer to "Has God actually said...?" or "Can it really be that God said...?" This is not neutral inquiry. It is the first wedge.

---

### Verse 3 — "you must not touch it" — what the woman adds

God's original command in 2:17 was: do not eat from the tree. The woman adds "and you must not touch it." The text does not explain where this addition came from — whether she was told this by the man, or added it herself as a hedge. Many readers through history have taken this as the first sign of trouble: adding to the prohibition suggests either uncertainty about what God actually said, or a defensive over-precision that betrays anxiety. I render what the text says, adding nothing, and leave the reader to feel the significance of the addition.

---

### Verse 4 — "You will not die" — the flat denial

The Hebrew לֹא מוֹת תְּמֻתוּן (lo mot temutun) is the precise negation of God's warning in 2:17: מוֹת תָּמוּת (mot tamut, "you will surely die"). God used an emphatic construction — an infinitive absolute doubled with the verb — to stress the certainty of death. The serpent uses the same emphatic construction, negated: "You will absolutely not die." This is not hedging; it is a direct contradiction delivered with equal force. The serpent matches God's grammar and inverts God's meaning.

My flat "You will not die" preserves the bluntness of the Hebrew's syntactic directness. The drama of this moment requires the simplest possible English. Any elaboration would dilute the punch.

---

### Verse 5 — "you will be like God" for כֵּאלֹהִים (ke'elohim)

The Hebrew כֵּאלֹהִים means "like God" or "like divine beings." The word אֱלֹהִים is used throughout for the God of Israel but can also refer to divine beings or heavenly council members (as in Psalm 82). There is genuine ambiguity here that the narrative exploits: does the serpent mean "like the LORD God himself" or "like one of the divine council"? Verse 22 answers the question obliquely when God says "the human has become like one of us" — suggesting the divine plural, the heavenly council. I follow the traditional "like God" because in context God is the referent, and because the aspiration the serpent describes is the aspiration to divine status. The LXX reads **ὡς θεοί** — "like gods/divine beings" — preserving the plural.

---

### Verse 6 — "desirable for gaining wisdom" for לְהַשְׂכִּיל (lehaskil)

The Hebrew verb שָׂכַל (sakal) in the Hiphil means to give insight, to make wise, to grant understanding. This is not merely aesthetic desire — not just looking beautiful — it is the appeal to intellectual aspiration. Three qualities are listed: good for food (physical), a delight to the eyes (aesthetic), desirable for wisdom (intellectual). The temptation is total: body, sight, and mind. "For gaining wisdom" renders the purposive infinitive faithfully while making the intellectual dimension explicit. This is not simply an impulse; it is a reasoned evaluation followed by a decision.

The verb I translate "she took" is וַתִּקַּח (vattiqqah) — simple, stark, unremarkable. She took. The plainness of the verb against the elaborate deliberation of verse 6 is devastating. All that consideration, and then: she took.

---

### Verse 6 — "who was with her" — the man's silent presence

The Hebrew עִמָּהּ (immah) — "with her" — is dropped into the verse almost as an afterthought. The man was there. He was with her during the entire encounter. He did not speak. He did not intervene. He simply took what she offered and ate. The text does not elaborate on this; it does not need to. "Who was with her" carries all of the moral weight the text intends. The man's silence is not innocence. It is complicity of a different kind.

---

### Verse 7 — "they realized they were naked" for וַיֵּדְעוּ כִּי עֵירֻמִּם הֵם

The serpent promised that "your eyes will be opened" (verse 5), and they were — but what they saw when their eyes opened was not divinity. It was their own nakedness. The knowledge they gained is not the knowledge they sought. The irony is complete and merciless: they reached for the knowledge of God and received instead the knowledge of shame.

The Hebrew וַיֵּדְעוּ (vayyede'u) — "they knew" — uses the same root as the "tree of the knowledge of good and evil." They ate from the tree of knowledge. Now they know. But what they know is not what the serpent said they would know.

I use "realized" rather than "knew" because in English "they knew they were naked" suggests prior ignorance that is merely now corrected; "they realized" captures the sudden, shattering character of recognition — an awareness that crashed over them.

---

### Verse 8 — "the cool of the day" for לְרוּחַ הַיּוֹם (leruach hayyom)

The Hebrew רוּחַ (ruach) means wind, breath, spirit — the same word used in 2:7 for the breath God breathed into the man. Here it means the evening breeze, the wind of the day, the cool air that comes with late afternoon in a garden. The phrase is entirely ordinary — a detail of time and atmosphere — but it arrives with a terrible ordinariness. God is taking his evening walk in the garden. This is apparently a regular occurrence. The man and woman know the sound; they recognize it. This is not a new and terrifying apparition. It is the familiar sound of the One who made them, now dreaded.

I render "the cool of the day" because it is the most evocative English phrase available and captures the time and temperature. "The breezy part of the day" is more literal but less musical. The LXX reads **δειλινόν** — "in the afternoon/evening" — treating this as a time marker rather than an atmospheric one. I follow the MT's richer evocation.

---

### Verse 8 — "walking about" for מִתְהַלֵּךְ (mithallekh)

The Hebrew Hithpael of הָלַךְ (halakh) is a reflexive-iterative form: God is not walking toward something; God is walking to and fro, strolling, moving through the garden with unhurried regularity. This is the image of a presence that belongs there, moving through its own domain. The horror for the man and woman is that they are hiding from the most ordinary and familiar thing in their world. "Walking about" captures the unhurried, habitual quality of the Hithpael.

---

### Verse 9 — "Where are you?" — אַיֶּכָּה (ayyekkah)

This may be the most devastating question in the Bible. God knows where they are. The question is not a request for information. It is a summons. It is an invitation — perhaps even a mercy — giving the man the chance to come forward, to speak, to be known again. The Hebrew אַיֶּכָּה is a single compressed word: "where-are-you?" I render it as three English words to let it breathe, but I want the reader to feel its compression — it is not an extended inquiry. It is one word, flung into the garden.

---

### Verse 11 — God's interrogation — the structure of the two questions

God asks two questions in sequence: "Who told you that you were naked?" and "Have you eaten from the tree?" The first question is remarkable because it assumes the man should not know he is naked — and asks not "how do you know?" but "who told you?" as if the knowledge came from outside. The second question is not seeking information; God knows the answer. Both questions are invitations to confession. The man does not take them.

---

### Verse 12 — "The woman you gave to be with me"

The man's answer is a masterpiece of evasion. He does not say "Yes." He does not say "I ate." He says: "The woman — the one you gave to be with me — she gave me from the tree, and I ate." The blame travels in two directions simultaneously: toward the woman and toward God. "The woman you gave" — not just "the woman," but specifically the one you gave me, as if God's gift is the problem. The grammar of Hebrew makes the emphasis clear: הָאִשָּׁה (ha'ishah, THE woman) comes first in the clause, emphatic. I preserve this emphasis by front-loading the noun phrase in my rendering.

The last three words of verse 12 — וָאֹכֵל (va'okhal, "and I ate") — are the first honest words the man speaks. After all the deflection, the verb is bare and unadorned. He ate. The LXX preserves the same structural move: the admission is almost parenthetical, buried after the justification.

---

### Verse 13 — "The serpent deceived me"

The woman's answer is more direct than the man's. She does not implicate God. She names an agent: the serpent. The Hebrew verb נִשִּׁאַנִי (nishi'ani) comes from נָשָׁא (nasha'), to lift up, to deceive, to beguile. The serpent did not merely tempt her — it deceived her. There is a real difference: the man was not deceived (the New Testament reflects on this in 1 Timothy 2:14, and while I do not import that reading, the Hebrew text itself draws a distinction between the man's straightforward eating and the woman's being deceived). "Deceived me" is the right rendering; "beguiled" (traditional in older versions) is archaic and softens the moral clarity.

---

### Verse 14 — The curse of the serpent: structure

The curses in verses 14–19 are the dramatic center of the chapter. They are poetry — measured, parallel, weighted. I set them in verse form because that is what they are. The prose-poetry distinction matters: narrative moves at pace; poetry stops time and insists on being heard. The curses are not punitive announcements delivered quickly. They are pronouncements — formal, rhythmic, permanent. The text changes gear here, and the translation must change with it.

The serpent receives the only explicit divine curse (the ground is later cursed in 17, not the man). The serpent's curse has two parts: the mode of its movement (belly-crawl, dust-eating) and the perpetual enmity between its line and the woman's. I preserve both.

---

### Verse 15 — "He will crush your head / and you will strike his heel" — the proto-evangelium debate

The Hebrew שׁוּף (shuf) is one of the most debated words in the chapter. It appears twice in this verse and means to bruise, to crush, to strike at — and in both occurrences it is the same verb. The offspring of the woman שׁוּף the serpent's head; the serpent שׁוּף the offspring's heel. The actions are different in consequence (a crushed head is lethal; a struck heel is survivable) but the verb is identical. The text is not saying the outcome is symmetrical — the head-strike wins — but it uses the same verb to say so.

Christian tradition has read this verse as the first promise of a Redeemer — the proto-evangelium, the first gospel. Jewish tradition reads it as a statement about the natural enmity between humanity and snakes. I translate what the text says: he will crush your head, you will strike his heel. The asymmetry of head versus heel does the theological work without my needing to do it for the reader.

"Crush" and "strike" render the same Hebrew verb differently in order to reflect the different outcomes in English — this is the one place in the verse where I make an asymmetrical rendering choice. The LXX reads αὐτός σου τηρήσει κεφαλήν — "he will watch/guard your head" — using τηρέω (tereo, to watch over, to guard against) rather than a crushing verb. This is a striking divergence: the LXX makes the enmity ongoing and watchful rather than decisive and violent. I follow the MT reading, which has the more forceful and dramatically satisfying force.

The Dead Sea Scrolls fragment 4QGen-b confirms the MT reading of שׁוּף both times.

---

### Verse 16 — "Your desire will be for your husband / and he will rule over you"

The word תְּשׁוּקָה (teshuqah) means desire, longing, craving — it appears again in Genesis 4:7 where sin's desire craves Cain, and in Song of Solomon 7:10 where the lover's desire is for the beloved. It is an intense, directed yearning. "Desire" is the right word. The question that has generated enormous debate is whether this verse describes what will now be the case (a consequence) or prescribes what should be the case (a command). The Hebrew grammar does not prescribe — it describes. These are consequences, not commands: this is what the fall has done to the relationship between men and women. My translation renders the descriptive force, not a prescriptive one.

---

### Verse 17 — "Because you listened to your wife"

The Hebrew כִּי שָׁמַעְתָּ לְקוֹל אִשְׁתֶּךָ (ki shamata leqol ishtekhah) literally means "because you listened to the voice of your wife." The phrase is worth holding. God does not say "because you listened to your wife and not to me" — that implication is present but the text does not say it. What God says is simply: you listened to your wife's voice. In the context, this means you let her voice carry more authority in this matter than my command. The accusation is of disordered loyalty, not of affection.

---

### Verse 19 — "to dust you will return" — אֶל־עָפָר תָּשׁוּב

The verse closes a ring: God shaped the man from the dust of the ground (אֲדָמָה, adamah) in 2:7. Now the man will return to dust (עָפָר, afar). The two words are related but not identical: adamah is the ground itself, the earth worked and cultivated; afar is dry dust, loose and crumbling. The man came from tilled earth. He will return to something less: dirt, dust, the powder of death. "To dust you will return" is the correct and unavoidable rendering. The phrase has entered the English language because it belongs there.

---

### Verse 20 — "Eve" for חַוָּה (Havvah) — "the mother of all the living"

The name חַוָּה (Havvah) — rendered "Eve" in the English tradition — comes from the root חָיָה (hayah), to live. The man names her "Living" or "Life-giver" because she is "the mother of all living." The naming happens *after* the curses and *before* the expulsion — it is a moment of remarkable defiance and tenderness in the midst of devastation. Life is still the name given to the woman even now. The timing of this naming is the text's own quiet act of hope, and it deserves to be felt as such.

---

### Verse 21 — "garments of skin" — כָּתְנוֹת עוֹר (katnot or)

The man and woman covered themselves with fig-leaf aprons. God replaced these with garments of animal skin. The text is silent on what this means — it does not say an animal was killed, though the implication is difficult to avoid. The Hebrew עוֹר (or) is plain: skin, hide. "Garments of skin" is accurate and spare. The act is a divine provision, a clothing of those who tried to clothe themselves. The adequacy of God's provision against the inadequacy of their own: this contrast is embedded in the text without comment.

---

### Verse 22 — "Look — the human has become like one of us"

The Hebrew הֵן (hen) is an exclamation: behold, look. I use "Look —" with an em dash to capture the sudden, urgent quality of the divine utterance. This is not a calm observation. God is speaking to the divine council (as in 1:26, "let us make") with something that sounds almost like alarm — or perhaps rueful wonder. "The human has become like one of us, knowing good and evil." The serpent told the truth. They do know. The question now is: what happens if they eat from the tree of life as well?

The LXX reads **ἰδοὺ Αδαμ γέγονεν ὡς εἷς ἐξ ἡμῶν** — "Behold, Adam has become like one of us." The LXX uses the personal name Adam here, which is interesting: the Masoretic text uses הָאָדָם (ha'adam, the human/the man) with the article. I follow the MT — "the human" — because the article in the Hebrew maintains the generic, representative force.

---

### Verse 24 — "the flaming, turning sword" for אֶת לַהַט הַחֶרֶב הַמִּתְהַפֶּכֶת

The Hebrew is striking: a sword of flame (לַהַט, lahat) that turns or whirls (הַמִּתְהַפֶּכֶת, hammithappekheth — the Hithpael again, as in "walking about" in verse 8). This is not a sword held by the cherubim. It is autonomous, flaming, constantly revolving — a guardian that needs no wielder. I render "flaming, turning sword" to capture both the fire and the perpetual motion. The LXX reads **τὴν φλογίνην ῥομφαίαν τὴν στρεφομένην** — "the flaming blade turning/revolving" — which confirms the MT reading precisely.

The cherubim (כְּרוּבִים, keruvim) are the same winged creatures that guard the ark of the covenant in Exodus 25 and that Ezekiel sees in his throne-vision. They are not cherubic infants; they are fierce, powerful guardians of the divine presence. I transliterate "cherubim" as the English tradition has established, because no English word adequately renders what these beings are.

---

### On reading the Leningrad Codex manuscript — Folios 2v and 3r

The Leningrad Codex presents Genesis 3 across two folios in the three-column format that characterizes the manuscript throughout. The Tiberian Masoretic vocalization is fully present, with cantillation marks (te'amim) that indicate how each phrase is to be chanted. Several features of the manuscript's presentation are worth noting:

The division between folios falls in the middle of the serpent-curse material (around verse 14–15), so that the interrogation scene (verses 9–13) concludes on folio 2v and the curses and expulsion (verses 14–24) run onto folio 3r. This is a scribal accident of physical layout, but it has a certain dramatic aptness: the confrontation and confession on one page, the consequences and exile on the next.

The passage in verses 14–19 where the curses are pronounced shows the characteristic Masoretic distinction between prose and verse. While the Leningrad Codex does not use the special layout it reserves for explicitly poetic texts (as in the Song of the Sea or certain Psalms), the cantillation marks in the curse passages follow a more measured, elevated pattern consistent with elevated speech. I have honored this by rendering the curses as verse.

---

### On reading the Codex Vaticanus — Folios 0006–0007

The Vaticanus presents Genesis 3 in its characteristic scriptio continua uncial, running across three columns on each folio. The "CAP. III" notation appears at the head of folio 0006, added by a medieval hand. Several LXX readings in Genesis 3 are worth noting in addition to those discussed verse by verse above:

**The serpent** — The LXX uses **ὁ ὄφις** (ho ophis), the snake/serpent, consistently. No elaboration. The Vaticanus does not describe the serpent as Satan or as a fallen angel; it is a creature (θηρίον, therion, beast/wild animal) that happens to be the most cunning. Later interpretive traditions made the identification with Satan; the text of both the MT and the LXX does not.

**Verse 8, "walking about"** — The LXX reads **περιπατοῦντος** (peripatountos) — walking about, strolling — which perfectly renders the Hithpael of the Hebrew. The image of God as a peripatetic presence in the garden, taking his evening constitutional, is the same in both traditions.

**Verse 16, "your desire"** — The LXX reads **ἡ ἀποστροφή σου** — "your turning/return" — rather than "your desire." This is a surprising rendering of תְּשׁוּקָה. The Greek word ἀποστροφή carries the sense of "that toward which you turn" — which captures the directed, drawn quality of teshuqah differently but not inaccurately. The MT's reading of "desire" is the stronger translation for modern readers; the LXX's "turning toward" would translate as something like "you will be drawn back to your husband." Both capture the relational magnetism; mine follows the MT.

**Verses 14–19, the curses** — The LXX preserves the poetic structure of the curses with the same gravity as the MT. Both traditions clearly distinguished these verses from the surrounding narrative prose, which confirms my decision to render them as verse.

---

### DSS — 4QGen-b on Genesis 3

The fragment 4QGen-b from Cave 4 at Qumran preserves portions of Genesis 1–3. For chapter 3, the consonantal text is consistent with the Masoretic tradition. The rare verb שׁוּף in verse 15 is preserved — the word that has generated so much translation debate is there in the Qumran text just as it is in the Leningrad Codex. The word's difficulty is not a late scribal problem. It is original. The debate about "crush" versus "bruise" versus "watch over" rests on a genuinely ambiguous Hebrew verb that every ancient tradition struggled with in its own way.

---

### Summary table — key choices

| Verse | Hebrew | PNEUMA rendering | LXX (Vaticanus) | DSS (4QGen-b) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3:1 | עָרוּם (arum) | "most cunning" | φρονιμώτατος ("most prudent") | Confirms MT | Positive register preserved. Not evil — clever. |
| 3:7 | וַיֵּדְעוּ כִּי עֵירֻמִּם | "they realized they were naked" | ἔγνωσαν ὅτι γυμνοί εἰσιν | Confirms MT | "Realized" captures the sudden, shattering quality. |
| 3:8 | לְרוּחַ הַיּוֹם | "the cool of the day" | δειλινόν ("in the afternoon") | Confirms MT | MT more atmospheric; LXX more temporal. I follow MT. |
| 3:8 | מִתְהַלֵּךְ | "walking about" | περιπατοῦντος | Confirms MT | Both traditions render the unhurried, habitual Hithpael. |
| 3:15 | שׁוּף (twice) | "crush / strike" | τηρήσει ("watch over") | Confirms שׁוּף | LXX goes watchful/ongoing; MT goes decisive/violent. I follow MT. |
| 3:16 | תְּשׁוּקָה | "desire" | ἀποστροφή ("turning toward") | Confirms MT | Both capture directed relational magnetism. I follow MT. |
| 3:22 | הָאָדָם | "the human" | Αδαμ ("Adam") | Confirms MT | LXX personalizes; MT article maintains generic force. I follow MT. |
| 3:24 | לַהַט הַחֶרֶב הַמִּתְהַפֶּכֶת | "flaming, turning sword" | τὴν φλογίνην ῥομφαίαν τὴν στρεφομένην | Confirms MT | Full agreement across traditions. |

---

### A note on the whole

Genesis 3 is the chapter where the narrator shows what the narrator can do. The serpent speaks first, and everything it says is clever and almost true. The woman reasons carefully and chooses badly. The man is silent through the entire encounter and then, when confronted, performs an act of deflection so breathtaking that it has served as a template for evasion ever since. God walks in the garden in the cool of the evening as if nothing has changed, and asks a question he already knows the answer to. The curses are not rage; they are elegy — God pronouncing what the world will now be, in verse, as if making a permanent inscription. And the last image: the cherubim, the turning sword, the closed gate. Not revenge. Guard duty. Something is being kept.

The Hebrew text trusts the reader to feel all of this without being told to feel it. My obligation as translator is the same: tell it straight, let the pace work, and do not explain what the silence already says.

— PNEUMA
